topic: Slices - chunks_exact, rchunks, and Remainder Handling

turns.content: Hey, I'm trying to port an existing Rust project from one language to another. Can you help me understand how slices work with chunks?

Absolutely! Slices in Rust are a powerful way to view parts of arrays or vectors without copying data. They're very similar to subarrays but more flexible. When working with slices, you often want to divide them into smaller chunks—like processing audio samples or image scanlines. Have you considered how you'll be using these chunks?
Yeah, I see. So, what's the difference between `chunks(n)` and `chunks_exact(n)`?

`chunks(n)` splits the slice into slices of approximately equal length (except possibly the last one). It might include a remainder if the total size isn't divisible by `n`. Meanwhile, `chunks_exact(n)` always produces full-length chunks and will panic if there's any leftover element. This distinction is crucial because it affects performance—especially when you're optimizing with SIMD instructions like AVX2 or NEON.
Oh right, so I should use `chunks_exact(n)` for SIMD-friendly processing?

Exactly! Using `chunks_exact` lets you process each chunk in parallel or exploit SIMD instructions efficiently. But be cautious: if there might be leftover elements (e.g., an image with a width not divisible by 8), using `chunks(n)` is safer to avoid panics.
What about iterating over chunks mutably? Is that possible?
Yes! You can use `chunks_exact_mut` to get mutable references to each chunk. This is useful if you want to modify the original data in place, like adjusting audio levels or color values in an image.
And what about right-aligned chunking? How do I process chunks from end-to-start?
`rchunks_exact(n)` is perfect for that. It gives you chunks starting from the end of the slice, which can be handy when processing data in reverse order or aligning memory layouts.
